title: Getting Started with ipf
text: Survey samples rarely match population perfectly key demographics. Raking (iterative proportional fitting) adjusts case weights weighted sample margins match known population targets. ipf makes fast using Rust perform computations. vignette walks complete raking workflow using bundled anes24 dataset, taken subset 2024 American National Election Study https://electionstudies.org/data-center/2024-time-series-study/.

title: Getting Started with ipf
text: data contains 966 respondents ANES 2024 face--face sample:
code: library(ipf) library(tibble) data(anes24) anes24 #> # A tibble: 966 × 7 #>    state sex    race     income     education    married  presidential #>    <chr> <chr>  <chr>    <chr>      <chr>        <chr>    <chr>        #>  1 CO    Female White    Over $100k NA           Divorced Trump        #>  2 CA    Male   White    Over $100k NA           Married  Harris       #>  3 NC    Female White    NA         Bachelor's   Married  Harris       #>  4 MA    Male   NA       Over $100k Some college Married  Trump        #>  5 WA    Female Black    NA         Bachelor's   Married  NA           #>  6 GA    Male   White    $50k-$100k NA           NA       Trump        #>  7 AR    Male   Black    Under $50k NA           NA       Harris       #>  8 TX    Male   Hispanic Under $50k Some college NA       Trump        #>  9 OR    Male   White    Under $50k NA           Divorced NA           #> 10 AL    Female White    Under $50k NA           Divorced NA           #> # ℹ 956 more rows

title: Getting Started with ipf
text: writing targets, inspect levels sample see values missing: define targets, target names must match data values exactly. default, NA values ignored variable raking. use na_method = 'bucket', ipf treats missing values implicit extra category preserves total weight rescaling named targets remaining nonmissing weight mass.
code: table(anes24$sex, useNA = 'ifany') #>  #> Female   Male   <NA>  #>    503    458      5 table(anes24$race, useNA = 'ifany') #>  #>    Asian    Black Hispanic    Other    White     <NA>  #>       28      118      114       63      632       11 table(anes24$income, useNA = 'ifany') #>  #> $50k-$100k Over $100k Under $50k       <NA>  #>        300        389        230         47

title: Getting Started with ipf
text: Targets named list named numeric vectors. vector’s names must match levels data, values proportions summing 1. targets don’t sum 1, ipf normalize automatically warning.
code: targets <- list(   sex = c(Male = 0.472, Female = 0.528),   race = c(     White = 0.706,     Black = 0.121,     Hispanic = 0.107,     Asian = 0.047,     Other = 0.019   ),   income = c(     'Under $50k' = 0.151,     '$50k-$100k' = 0.294,     'Over $100k' = 0.555   ) )

title: Getting Started with ipf
text: main function rake(): result ipf_rake object containing weights diagnostics. want missing values raking variable act like implicit category, use na_method = 'bucket': already design weights, pass base_weights:
code: result <- rake(anes24, targets, cap = NULL) result #>  #> ── Raking result (ipf) #> Converged: Yes (5 iterations, max prop err = 3.04e-07) #> Variables raked: "sex", "race", and "income" #> Missing handling: "exclude" #> Design effect: 1.113 | Effective n: 868 / 966 #> Weight range: [0.199, 1.928] | Mean: 1 | SD: 0.337 bucketed <- rake(anes24, targets, cap = NULL, na_method = 'bucket') bucketed #>  #> ── Raking result (ipf) #> Converged: Yes (6 iterations, max prop err = 5.46e-07) #> Variables raked: "sex", "race", and "income" #> Missing handling: "bucket" #> Design effect: 1.114 | Effective n: 867 / 966 #> Weight range: [0.199, 1.936] | Mean: 1 | SD: 0.337 base_w <- ifelse(anes24$sex == 'Female', 1.1, 0.9) base_w[is.na(base_w)] <- 1  base_weighted <- rake(anes24, targets, base_weights = base_w, cap = NULL) base_weighted #>  #> ── Raking result (ipf) #> Converged: Yes (5 iterations, max prop err = 3.04e-07) #> Variables raked: "sex", "race", and "income" #> Missing handling: "exclude" #> Design effect: 1.113 | Effective n: 868 / 966 #> Weight range: [0.199, 1.928] | Mean: 1 | SD: 0.337

title: Getting Started with ipf
text: design effect measures much weighting inflates variance. deff 1.0 means inflation (uniform weights). Higher values mean less effective data.
code: design_effect(result$weights) #> $deff #> [1] 1.113407 #>  #> $n_eff #> [1] 867.6076

title: Getting Started with ipf
text: summary() shows full diagnostic report: residual discrepancy column shows close weighted distribution target.
code: summary(result) #>  #> ── Raking Summary (ipf) #> ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #> ✔ Converged in 5 iterations (max prop err = 3.04e-07) #> ℹ No base weights (uniform) #> ℹ Selection: type = "nolim", method = "total" #> ℹ Missing handling: "exclude" #> ℹ Variables raked: "sex", "race", and "income" #> ── Weight Summary ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #> Min: 0.1993 Q1: 0.6688 Median: 0.9703 Mean: 1 Q3: 1.2794 Max: 1.9275 #> SD: 0.3368 CV: 0.3368 #> ── Design Effect ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #> Deff: 1.1134 | Effective n: 868 / 966 #> ── Per-Variable Assessment ───────────────────────────────────────────────────── #>  #> ── sex #> # A tibble: 3 × 9 #>   level  target unweighted_n unweighted_pct weighted_n weighted_pct change_pct #>   <chr>   <dbl>        <dbl>          <dbl>      <dbl>        <dbl>      <dbl> #> 1 Male    0.472          458          0.477       454.        0.472   -0.00459 #> 2 Female  0.528          503          0.523       508.        0.528    0.00459 #> 3 Total   1              961          1           962.        1.00     0.00917 #> # ℹ 2 more variables: residual_disc <dbl>, original_disc <dbl> #>  #> ── race #> # A tibble: 6 × 9 #>   level    target unweighted_n unweighted_pct weighted_n weighted_pct change_pct #>   <chr>     <dbl>        <dbl>          <dbl>      <dbl>        <dbl>      <dbl> #> 1 White     0.706          632         0.662       674.        0.706     0.0442  #> 2 Black     0.121          118         0.124       116.        0.121    -0.00256 #> 3 Hispanic  0.107          114         0.119       102.        0.107    -0.0124  #> 4 Asian     0.047           28         0.0293       44.9       0.0470    0.0177  #> 5 Other     0.019           63         0.0660       18.1       0.0190   -0.0470  #> 6 Total     1              955         1           955.        1.00      0.124   #> # ℹ 2 more variables: residual_disc <dbl>, original_disc <dbl> #>  #> ── income #> # A tibble: 4 × 9 #>   level    target unweighted_n unweighted_pct weighted_n weighted_pct change_pct #>   <chr>     <dbl>        <dbl>          <dbl>      <dbl>        <dbl>      <dbl> #> 1 Under $…  0.151          230          0.250       139.        0.151    -0.0993 #> 2 $50k-$1…  0.294          300          0.326       270.        0.294    -0.0324 #> 3 Over $1…  0.555          389          0.423       509.        0.555     0.132  #> 4 Total     1              919          1           917.        1.00      0.263  #> # ℹ 2 more variables: residual_disc <dbl>, original_disc <dbl>

title: Getting Started with ipf
text: programmatic use, broom-style methods return tibbles:
code: # One row per variable-level tidy(result) #> # A tibble: 10 × 5 #>    variable level      target weighted_pct discrepancy #>    <chr>    <chr>       <dbl>        <dbl>       <dbl> #>  1 sex      Male        0.472       0.472     1.43e- 7 #>  2 sex      Female      0.528       0.528    -1.43e- 7 #>  3 race     White       0.706       0.706    -3.36e- 9 #>  4 race     Black       0.121       0.121     6.71e- 9 #>  5 race     Hispanic    0.107       0.107    -2.10e- 9 #>  6 race     Asian       0.047       0.0470   -1.69e- 9 #>  7 race     Other       0.019       0.0190    4.44e-10 #>  8 income   Under $50k  0.151       0.151     3.05e-16 #>  9 income   $50k-$100k  0.294       0.294     1.11e-16 #> 10 income   Over $100k  0.555       0.555    -2.55e-15  # One-row summary glance(result) #> # A tibble: 1 × 7 #>   converged iterations max_prop_err  deff n_eff n_obs n_vars #>   <lgl>          <int>        <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <int>  <int> #> 1 TRUE               5  0.000000304  1.11  868.   966      3

title: Getting Started with ipf
text: use weights downstream analyses, attach data: .weight column can used downstream analyses. example, can compare estimate weighting:
code: weighted_data <- augment(result) head(weighted_data) #> # A tibble: 6 × 8 #>   state sex    race  income     education    married  presidential .weight #>   <chr> <chr>  <chr> <chr>      <chr>        <chr>    <chr>          <dbl> #> 1 CO    Female White Over $100k NA           Divorced Trump          1.37  #> 2 CA    Male   White Over $100k NA           Married  Harris         1.28  #> 3 NC    Female White NA         Bachelor's   Married  Harris         1.07  #> 4 MA    Male   NA    Over $100k Some college Married  Trump          1.23  #> 5 WA    Female Black NA         Bachelor's   Married  NA             1.12  #> 6 GA    Male   White $50k-$100k NA           NA       Trump          0.908 presidential_data <- subset(weighted_data, !is.na(presidential))  presidential_unweighted <- prop.table(table(presidential_data$presidential))  presidential_weighted <- aggregate(   .weight ~ presidential,   presidential_data,   sum ) presidential_weighted$weighted_pct <- presidential_weighted$.weight /   sum(presidential_weighted$.weight)  presidential_compare <- tibble::tibble(   presidential = presidential_weighted$presidential,   unweighted_pct = as.numeric(presidential_unweighted[     presidential_weighted$presidential   ]),   weighted_pct = presidential_weighted$weighted_pct )  presidential_compare #> # A tibble: 2 × 3 #>   presidential unweighted_pct weighted_pct #>   <chr>                 <dbl>        <dbl> #> 1 Harris                0.564        0.575 #> 2 Trump                 0.436        0.425

title: Getting Started with ipf
text: default, weights capped 5. Tighter bounds reduce extreme weights can leave residual mismatch.
code: # Unbounded fit from above range(result$weights) #> [1] 0.1993456 1.9275117 design_effect(result$weights) #> $deff #> [1] 1.113407 #>  #> $n_eff #> [1] 867.6076  # Default cap default_bounded <- rake(anes24, targets) range(default_bounded$weights) #> [1] 0.1993456 1.9275117 design_effect(default_bounded$weights) #> $deff #> [1] 1.113407 #>  #> $n_eff #> [1] 867.6076  # Tighter cap tight <- rake(anes24, targets, cap = 3) range(tight$weights) #> [1] 0.1993456 1.9275117 design_effect(tight$weights) #> $deff #> [1] 1.113407 #>  #> $n_eff #> [1] 867.6076  # Or specify both min and max bounds bounded <- rake(anes24, targets, bounds = c(0.3, 3)) range(bounded$weights) #> [1] 0.300000 1.950523

title: Getting Started with ipf
text: many potential raking variables, can let ipf select discrepant ones: Use type = 'nlim' select top N discrepant variables, iterate = TRUE re-check round add newly discrepant variables.
code: targets_many <- list(   sex = c(Male = 0.472, Female = 0.528),   race = c(     White = 0.706,     Black = 0.121,     Hispanic = 0.107,     Asian = 0.047,     Other = 0.019   ),   income = c('Under $50k' = 0.151, '$50k-$100k' = 0.294, 'Over $100k' = 0.555),   married = c(     Married = 0.58,     Widowed = 0.06,     Divorced = 0.10,     Separated = 0.01,     'Never married' = 0.25   ) )  # Only rake on variables where discrepancy exceeds 5% result_pct <- rake(anes24, targets_many, type = 'pctlim', pctlim = 0.05) result_pct$vars_used #> [1] "race"    "income"  "married" "sex"

title: Getting Started with ipf
text: can inspect raw discrepancy scores without raking:
code: find_discrepant_vars(   anes24,   targets_many,   weights = rep(1, nrow(anes24)),   choosemethod = 'total' ) #>         sex        race      income     married  #> 0.009173777 0.123801047 0.263427639 0.483570392

what: Iterative Proportional Fitting (Raking) with a Rust Backend
title: Iterative Proportional Fitting (Raking) with a Rust Backend
text: ipf provides fast iterative proportional fitting (raking) survey weighting. computational core written Rust speed numerical stability. supports multiple raking variables, automatic variable selection, weight bounding, comprehensive diagnostics.

title: Iterative Proportional Fitting (Raking) with a Rust Backend
text: can install development version ipf GitHub :
code: # install.packages("pak") pak::pak("christopherkenny/ipf")

title: Iterative Proportional Fitting (Raking) with a Rust Backend
text: Rake survey sample match known population targets: Examine per-variable diagnostics summary(): Extract weights downstream analysis: Get tidy one-row-per-level view: single-row model summary:
code: library(ipf) data(anes24)  # Define population targets as named proportions targets <- list(   sex = c(Male = 0.48, Female = 0.52),   race = c(     White = 0.59,     Black = 0.14,     Hispanic = 0.19,     Asian = 0.06,     Other = 0.02   ) )  # Rake result <- rake(anes24, targets) result #>  #> ── Raking result (ipf) #> Converged: Yes (3 iterations, max prop err = 8.02e-08) #> Variables raked: "sex" and "race" #> Missing handling: "exclude" #> Design effect: 1.115 | Effective n: 867 / 966 #> Weight range: [0.301, 2.056] | Mean: 1 | SD: 0.339 summary(result) #>  #> ── Raking Summary (ipf) #> ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #> ✔ Converged in 3 iterations (max prop err = 8.02e-08) #> ℹ No base weights (uniform) #> ℹ Selection: type = "nolim", method = "total" #> ℹ Missing handling: "exclude" #> ℹ Variables raked: "sex" and "race" #> ── Weight Summary ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #> Min: 0.301 Q1: 0.8853 Median: 0.8982 Mean: 1 Q3: 1.1263 Max: 2.056 #> SD: 0.3386 CV: 0.3386 #> ── Design Effect ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #> Deff: 1.1146 | Effective n: 867 / 966 #> ── Per-Variable Assessment ───────────────────────────────────────────────────── #>  #> ── sex #> # A tibble: 3 × 9 #>   level  target unweighted_n unweighted_pct weighted_n weighted_pct change_pct #>   <chr>   <dbl>        <dbl>          <dbl>      <dbl>        <dbl>      <dbl> #> 1 Male     0.48          458          0.477       461.        0.480    0.00341 #> 2 Female   0.52          503          0.523       500.        0.520   -0.00341 #> 3 Total    1             961          1           961.        1.000    0.00683 #> # ℹ 2 more variables: residual_disc <dbl>, original_disc <dbl> #>  #> ── race #> # A tibble: 6 × 9 #>   level    target unweighted_n unweighted_pct weighted_n weighted_pct change_pct #>   <chr>     <dbl>        <dbl>          <dbl>      <dbl>        <dbl>      <dbl> #> 1 White      0.59          632         0.662       563.        0.590     -0.0718 #> 2 Black      0.14          118         0.124       134.        0.140      0.0164 #> 3 Hispanic   0.19          114         0.119       181.        0.190      0.0706 #> 4 Asian      0.06           28         0.0293       57.3       0.0600     0.0307 #> 5 Other      0.02           63         0.0660       19.1       0.0200    -0.0460 #> 6 Total      1             955         1           955.        1.000      0.235  #> # ℹ 2 more variables: residual_disc <dbl>, original_disc <dbl> augmented <- augment(result) augmented[, c("sex", "race", ".weight")] #> # A tibble: 966 × 3 #>    sex    race     .weight #>    <chr>  <chr>      <dbl> #>  1 Female White      0.885 #>  2 Male   White      0.898 #>  3 Female White      0.885 #>  4 Male   <NA>       1.01  #>  5 Female Black      1.13  #>  6 Male   White      0.898 #>  7 Male   Black      1.14  #>  8 Male   Hispanic   1.60  #>  9 Male   White      0.898 #> 10 Female White      0.885 #> # ℹ 956 more rows tidy(result) #> # A tibble: 7 × 5 #>   variable level    target weighted_pct discrepancy #>   <chr>    <chr>     <dbl>        <dbl>       <dbl> #> 1 sex      Male       0.48       0.480     3.85e- 8 #> 2 sex      Female     0.52       0.520    -3.85e- 8 #> 3 race     White      0.59       0.590     2.89e-15 #> 4 race     Black      0.14       0.140     5.00e-16 #> 5 race     Hispanic   0.19       0.190     5.27e-16 #> 6 race     Asian      0.06       0.0600    3.05e-16 #> 7 race     Other      0.02       0.0200    5.90e-17 glance(result) #> # A tibble: 1 × 7 #>   converged iterations max_prop_err  deff n_eff n_obs n_vars #>   <lgl>          <int>        <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <int>  <int> #> 1 TRUE               3 0.0000000802  1.11  867.   966      2

title: ANES 2024 Time Series Study (subset) — anes24
text: subset 2024 American National Election Study (ANES) Time Series face--face sample, containing demographic vote choice variables 966 respondents. Useful demonstrating survey raking workflows.

title: ANES 2024 Time Series Study (subset) — anes24
code: anes24

title: ANES 2024 Time Series Study (subset) — anes24
text: tibble 966 rows 7 columns: state Two-letter US state abbreviation. NA respondents whose state identified (106 missing). sex Respondent sex: "Male" "Female" (5 missing). race Race/ethnicity: "White", "Black", "Hispanic", "Asian", "" (11 missing). income Household income bracket: "$50k", "$50k-$100k", "$100k" (47 missing). education Education: "Less HS", "High school", "college", "Bachelor's", "Graduate" (451 missing). married Marital status: "Married", "Widowed", "Divorced", "Separated", "Never married" (277 missing). presidential 2024 presidential vote choice: "Harris" "Trump" (335 missing).

title: ANES 2024 Time Series Study (subset) — anes24
text: https://electionstudies.org/data-center/2024-time-series-study/

title: ANES 2024 Time Series Study (subset) — anes24
text: American National Election Studies. 2025. ANES 2024 Time Series Study Full Release (dataset documentation). August 8, 2025 version. https://www.electionstudies.org/

what: Augment data with raked weights — augment.ipf_rake
title: Augment data with raked weights — augment.ipf_rake
text: Returns original data frame .weight column appended.

title: Augment data with raked weights — augment.ipf_rake
code: # S3 method for class 'ipf_rake' augment(x, ...)

title: Augment data with raked weights — augment.ipf_rake
text: x ipf_rake object. ... Additional arguments (ignored).

title: Augment data with raked weights — augment.ipf_rake
text: tibble original columns plus .weight.

title: Augment data with raked weights — augment.ipf_rake
code: data <- data.frame(   gender = sample(c('M', 'F'), 100, replace = TRUE, prob = c(0.6, 0.4)) ) targets <- list(gender = c(M = 0.5, F = 0.5)) result <- rake(data, targets) augment(result) #> # A tibble: 100 × 2 #>    gender .weight #>    <chr>    <dbl> #>  1 M        0.758 #>  2 F        1.47  #>  3 F        1.47  #>  4 M        0.758 #>  5 M        0.758 #>  6 M        0.758 #>  7 M        0.758 #>  8 M        0.758 #>  9 F        1.47  #> 10 F        1.47  #> # ℹ 90 more rows

what: Compute design effect and effective sample size — design_effect
title: Compute design effect and effective sample size — design_effect
text: design effect (deff) measures variance inflation factor due unequal weighting. effective sample size n / deff.

title: Compute design effect and effective sample size — design_effect
code: design_effect(weights)

title: Compute design effect and effective sample size — design_effect
text: weights Numeric weight vector.

title: Compute design effect and effective sample size — design_effect
text: list deff (design effect) n_eff (effective sample size).

title: Compute design effect and effective sample size — design_effect

what: Find discrepant variables and their aggregate discrepancy scores — find_discrepant_vars
title: Find discrepant variables and their aggregate discrepancy scores — find_discrepant_vars
text: Calculates discrepancy current weighted distribution target distributions variable, aggregates using chosen method.

title: Find discrepant variables and their aggregate discrepancy scores — find_discrepant_vars
code: find_discrepant_vars(   data,   targets,   weights,   choosemethod = "total",   na_method = c("exclude", "bucket") )

title: Find discrepant variables and their aggregate discrepancy scores — find_discrepant_vars
text: data Data frame. targets Named list named numeric target vectors (proportions). weights Numeric weight vector. choosemethod Method aggregating per-category discrepancies. One "total", "max", "average", "totalsquared", "maxsquared", "averagesquared". na_method handle NA values. "exclude" skips NA cases margin. "bucket" treats missing values implicit extra category.

title: Find discrepant variables and their aggregate discrepancy scores — find_discrepant_vars
text: Named numeric vector aggregate discrepancy per variable.

title: Find discrepant variables and their aggregate discrepancy scores — find_discrepant_vars
code: data <- data.frame(   gender = sample(c('M', 'F'), 100, replace = TRUE, prob = c(0.6, 0.4)),   age = sample(c('young', 'old'), 100, replace = TRUE, prob = c(0.7, 0.3)) ) targets <- list(   gender = c(M = 0.5, F = 0.5),   age = c(young = 0.6, old = 0.4) ) find_discrepant_vars(data, targets, weights = rep(1, 100)) #> gender    age  #>   0.00   0.16

title: Glance at an ipf_rake object — glance.ipf_rake
code: # S3 method for class 'ipf_rake' glance(x, ...)

title: Glance at an ipf_rake object — glance.ipf_rake
text: x ipf_rake object. ... Additional arguments (ignored).

title: Glance at an ipf_rake object — glance.ipf_rake
text: single-row tibble columns: converged, iterations, max_prop_err, deff, n_eff, n_obs, n_vars.

title: Glance at an ipf_rake object — glance.ipf_rake
code: data <- data.frame(   gender = sample(c('M', 'F'), 100, replace = TRUE, prob = c(0.6, 0.4)) ) targets <- list(gender = c(M = 0.5, F = 0.5)) result <- rake(data, targets) glance(result) #> # A tibble: 1 × 7 #>   converged iterations max_prop_err  deff n_eff n_obs n_vars #>   <lgl>          <int>        <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <int>  <int> #> 1 TRUE               1     3.33e-16  1.00  99.8   100      1

what: ipf: Iterative Proportional Fitting (Raking) with a Rust Backend — ipf-package
title: ipf: Iterative Proportional Fitting (Raking) with a Rust Backend — ipf-package
text: Fast iterative proportional fitting (raking) survey weighting. computational core written Rust speed numerical stability. Supports multiple raking variables, automatic variable selection, weight bounding, comprehensive diagnostics.

title: ipf: Iterative Proportional Fitting (Raking) with a Rust Backend — ipf-package
text: Maintainer: Christopher T. Kenny christopherkenny@fas.harvard.edu (ORCID)

text: Print ipf_rake object

title: Print an ipf_rake object — print.ipf_rake
code: # S3 method for class 'ipf_rake' print(x, ...)

title: Print an ipf_rake object — print.ipf_rake
text: x ipf_rake object. ... Additional arguments (ignored).

title: Print an ipf_rake object — print.ipf_rake
text: Invisibly returns x.

title: Print an ipf_rake object — print.ipf_rake
code: data <- data.frame(   gender = sample(c('M', 'F'), 100, replace = TRUE, prob = c(0.6, 0.4)) ) targets <- list(gender = c(M = 0.5, F = 0.5)) result <- rake(data, targets) print(result) #>  #> ── Raking result (ipf)  #> Converged: Yes (1 iterations, max prop err = 4.44e-16) #> Variables raked: "gender" #> Missing handling: "exclude" #> Design effect: 1.085 | Effective n: 92 / 100 #> Weight range: [0.781, 1.389] | Mean: 1 | SD: 0.292

title: Iterative proportional fitting (raking) — rake
text: Adjusts survey weights weighted marginal distributions match known population targets. Supports automatic variable selection, iterative re-raking, weight bounding.

title: Iterative proportional fitting (raking) — rake
code: rake(   data,   targets,   base_weights = NULL,   cap = 5,   bounds = NULL,   type = c("nolim", "pctlim", "nlim"),   pctlim = 0.05,   nlim = 5L,   choosemethod = c("total", "max", "average", "totalsquared", "maxsquared",     "averagesquared"),   na_method = c("exclude", "bucket"),   iterate = TRUE,   max_iter = 1000L,   tol = 1e-06,   verbose = FALSE,   diagnostics_every = 0L )

title: Iterative proportional fitting (raking) — rake
text: data data frame tibble containing survey data. targets named list named numeric vectors specifying target proportions raking variable. Names list must match column names data. vector's names must match levels corresponding variable. Values sum 1 (proportions); , normalized warning. base_weights Optional numeric vector base (design) weights. NULL (default), uniform weights 1 used. Centered mean 1 raking. cap Maximum weight value (ratio cap). Weights exceeding value trimmed weights renormalized. Default 5. Ignored bounds specified. bounds Optional numeric vector length 2, c(lo, hi), specifying minimum maximum weight bounds. Overrides cap. type Variable selection method: "nolim" (default): use variables targets. "pctlim": use variables discrepancy >= pctlim. "nlim": use nlim discrepant variables. pctlim Discrepancy threshold type = "pctlim". Default 0.05 (5 percentage points). nlim Number variables type = "nlim". Default 5. choosemethod Method aggregating per-category discrepancies single variable score. One "total", "max", "average", "totalsquared", "maxsquared", "averagesquared". na_method handle NA values raking variables. "exclude" (default): targets proportions among non-NA cases ; NA cases invisible margin. Matches anesrake. "bucket": NAs become frozen extra category; total weight preserved named targets rescaled remaining non-NA mass. iterate Logical. TRUE type = "pctlim", re-check discrepancies raking add newly discrepant variables, repeating 10 times. Default TRUE. max_iter Maximum number raking iterations. Default 1000. tol Convergence tolerance (max proportional error). Default 1e-6. verbose Logical. TRUE, print iteration progress. Default FALSE. diagnostics_every Record per-margin diagnostics every k iterations. 0 means baseline. Default 0.

title: Iterative proportional fitting (raking) — rake
text: ipf_rake object (S3 class) containing: weights: final raked weight vector data: input data frame converged: logical iterations: number iterations max_prop_err: final max proportional error targets: normalized targets used vars_used: character vector variables raked base_weights: original base weights type, choosemethod, na_method, cap: settings used deff, n_eff: design effect effective sample size diagnostics: tibble per-iteration diagnostics

title: Iterative proportional fitting (raking) — rake
code: data <- data.frame(   gender = sample(c('M', 'F'), 100, replace = TRUE, prob = c(0.6, 0.4)),   age = sample(c('young', 'old'), 100, replace = TRUE, prob = c(0.7, 0.3)) ) targets <- list(   gender = c(M = 0.5, F = 0.5),   age = c(young = 0.6, old = 0.4) ) result <- rake(data, targets) print(result) #>  #> ── Raking result (ipf)  #> Converged: Yes (2 iterations, max prop err = 3.13e-08) #> Variables raked: "gender" and "age" #> Missing handling: "exclude" #> Design effect: 1.074 | Effective n: 93 / 100 #> Weight range: [0.735, 1.55] | Mean: 1 | SD: 0.272

what: Objects exported from other packages — reexports
title: Objects exported from other packages — reexports
text: objects imported packages. Follow links see documentation. generics augment, glance, tidy

title: Summarize an ipf_rake object — summary.ipf_rake
text: Produces detailed summary including per-variable diagnostic tables showing target vs. achieved distributions.

title: Summarize an ipf_rake object — summary.ipf_rake
code: # S3 method for class 'ipf_rake' summary(object, ...)

title: Summarize an ipf_rake object — summary.ipf_rake
text: object ipf_rake object. ... Additional arguments (ignored).

title: Summarize an ipf_rake object — summary.ipf_rake
text: Invisibly returns list convergence info, weight summary, design effect, per-variable assessment tibbles.

title: Summarize an ipf_rake object — summary.ipf_rake
code: data <- data.frame(   gender = sample(c('M', 'F'), 100, replace = TRUE, prob = c(0.6, 0.4)) ) targets <- list(gender = c(M = 0.5, F = 0.5)) result <- rake(data, targets) summary(result) #>  #> ── Raking Summary (ipf)  #> ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #> ✔ Converged in 1 iterations (max prop err = 4.44e-16) #> ℹ No base weights (uniform) #> ℹ Selection: type = "nolim", method = "total" #> ℹ Missing handling: "exclude" #> ℹ Variables raked: "gender" #> ── Weight Summary ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #> Min: 0.8333 Q1: 0.8333 Median: 0.8333 Mean: 1 Q3: 1.25 Max: 1.25 #> SD: 0.2041 CV: 0.2041 #> ── Design Effect ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #> Deff: 1.0417 | Effective n: 96 / 100 #> ── Per-Variable Assessment ───────────────────────────────────────────────────── #>  #> ── gender  #> # A tibble: 3 × 9 #>   level target unweighted_n unweighted_pct weighted_n weighted_pct change_pct #>   <chr>  <dbl>        <dbl>          <dbl>      <dbl>        <dbl>      <dbl> #> 1 M        0.5           60            0.6         50        0.500    -0.1000 #> 2 F        0.5           40            0.4         50        0.500     0.100  #> 3 Total    1            100            1          100        1.00      0.200  #> # ℹ 2 more variables: residual_disc <dbl>, original_disc <dbl> #>

title: Tidy an ipf_rake object — tidy.ipf_rake
text: Returns one-row-per-variable-per-level tibble target proportions, weighted proportions, discrepancy.

title: Tidy an ipf_rake object — tidy.ipf_rake
code: # S3 method for class 'ipf_rake' tidy(x, ...)

title: Tidy an ipf_rake object — tidy.ipf_rake
text: x ipf_rake object. ... Additional arguments (ignored).

title: Tidy an ipf_rake object — tidy.ipf_rake
text: tibble columns: variable, level, target, weighted_pct, discrepancy.

title: Tidy an ipf_rake object — tidy.ipf_rake
code: data <- data.frame(   gender = sample(c('M', 'F'), 100, replace = TRUE, prob = c(0.6, 0.4)) ) targets <- list(gender = c(M = 0.5, F = 0.5)) result <- rake(data, targets) tidy(result) #> # A tibble: 2 × 5 #>   variable level target weighted_pct discrepancy #>   <chr>    <chr>  <dbl>        <dbl>       <dbl> #> 1 gender   M        0.5        0.500    8.33e-16 #> 2 gender   F        0.5        0.500   -3.33e-16

what: Assess weight quality with diagnostic tables — weight_assess
title: Assess weight quality with diagnostic tables — weight_assess
text: Produces per-variable diagnostic table comparing target distributions unweighted weighted distributions.

title: Assess weight quality with diagnostic tables — weight_assess
code: weight_assess(   data,   targets,   weights,   base_weights = NULL,   na_method = c("exclude", "bucket") )

title: Assess weight quality with diagnostic tables — weight_assess
text: data Data frame. targets Named list named numeric target vectors (proportions). weights Final raked weight vector. base_weights Original base weights raking. NULL, uses uniform weights. na_method handle NA values. "exclude" skips NA cases margin. "bucket" treats missing values implicit extra category.

title: Assess weight quality with diagnostic tables — weight_assess
text: Named list tibbles, one per variable.

title: Assess weight quality with diagnostic tables — weight_assess
code: data <- data.frame(   gender = sample(c('M', 'F'), 100, replace = TRUE, prob = c(0.6, 0.4)) ) targets <- list(gender = c(M = 0.5, F = 0.5)) result <- rake(data, targets) weight_assess(data, targets, result$weights) #> $gender #> # A tibble: 3 × 9 #>   level target unweighted_n unweighted_pct weighted_n weighted_pct change_pct #>   <chr>  <dbl>        <dbl>          <dbl>      <dbl>        <dbl>      <dbl> #> 1 M        0.5           59           0.59         50          0.5      -0.09 #> 2 F        0.5           41           0.41         50          0.5       0.09 #> 3 Total    1            100           1           100          1         0.18 #> # ℹ 2 more variables: residual_disc <dbl>, original_disc <dbl> #>
